sentences.text: Good morning.
My name is Stefan Stok.
That's Stefan, S-T-E-F-A-N and Stok, S-T-O-K.
I give as well a middle name, Alexander.
Alexander, A-L-E-X-A-N-D-E-R.

No, not really, except for the name that we used after the war in Poland in the years between 1945 and 1950, which was Stokowski or Stokstokowski, which was practically the same name with addition of suffix.
Just so that it would sound more Polish.
27 of August, 1933.
And what's the name of the city and the country that you were born?
Warsaw, Poland.
Warsaw, W-A-R-S-A-W.
In Poland, years, capital of Poland, in fact.
Right.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in Warsaw, pretty much in the central part of Warsaw, and in an apartment in the very nice house in Alberta Street.
And my father was a lawyer, and he was practicing from home.
That means that he had-- his office was in the apartment.

And I must say that the whole period before the war is-- I remember only certain sections, certain-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there are certain strong memories, but there are as well whole periods which are not entirely clear.
And in fact, things became much vivid, much more clear once the war started.
I was the only child.
And I was born to a pretty well off family, which was quite assimilated.
That means that my parents, that their language at home was Polish.

And there was practically [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all our friends and so on-- all the friends of my parents rather-- were belonged to this section of the Jewish community in Warsaw, which one would call intelligentsia, which were basically people who were mostly professionals and who basically had not very much contact or not very much connection with the orthodox or traditional Jewish community.
And my parents were-- they came from two different-- two different backgrounds.
My father's background was the Stok family, was a much more-- much more secular family in most ways.
And they were-- they originally lived in the East of Poland, not in Warsaw but in Brzesc nad Bugiem, which is now Brest-Litovsk, which is now there is a border state between Belarus and Poland.
And they had-- they were industrialists.
They had factories-- of soap factories.
And I am not quite sure when they were started, but sometime in the 19th century there was a firm of my grandfather and his brother-- brothers-- [POLISH] Stok.
And my grandfather then moved sometime in the late 19th century to Warsaw and had four children, among them, my father.
He died, unfortunately, quite early, and he died before I was born in 1932.
My grandfather, yes.
And after he died and after one of the son-- the oldest son-- who was-- who was more or less managing the factory after him-- after he became ill and then died, he died as well.
He died very young because of heart attack when he was not yet 40.
And the factory was then sold already before the war in the 1930s.
And they were-- the whole Stok family were very well off, very assimilated family, educated.
And although they kept the tradition in so far that they were celebrating the holidays-- Jewish holidays-- and so on, they had not otherwise any traditional leanings.
My family from my mother's side was very different, because that was much more traditional Jewish family, came from Kalisz in Western Poland.
And there was a really big family of 12 children, my mother among them.
Five brothers and seven sisters.
And the parents were orthodox, particularly the father was orthodox.
He was Hasid, and he belonged to the Hasidim here.
I always remember him in a more or less traditional Jewish clothes, in kind of a long black coat or-- and rather and-- with a long beard.
And the whole house was-- and he was, of course, completely orthodox, although to me he had to speak Polish because he couldn't speak to me Yiddish, as he spoke to his wife.
But all of the children were--
Because I didn't speak Yiddish, that's right.
But all the children were-- apart from the maybe two oldest-- all of them didn't want to remain traditional, and they more or less rebelled.
And they always wanted to study in the first place.
And indeed of my-- of the brothers and sisters of my mother, most of them went to university, to higher education.
And most of them basically rebelled against the traditional background and wanted to become-- to be part of the more modern society.
Right.

So we were living-- I spent all my first six years of my life in Warsaw in this apartment in Alberta, which was very comfortable, although I and-- apart from my parents and myself, there was-- there were-- there was a maid and nanny who was looking after me.
And that nanny first was Russian-- to the nanny that I think was in my early childhood was Russian.
And I think I spoke then Russian more than I spoke Polish to start.
And later there was another maid or another nanny who remained with me practically until the war.
Well, there was a maid who was as well-- who was as well a cook and there was my nanny.
I think that's about it.
No, my mother didn't work.

My mother-- actually, my mother was still-- I mean, she was studying in the university, and I think she interrupted her studies for a year when I was-- or something like that-- when I was born, and then she had to complete her studies so that took her until probably something like two years after I was born.
But she didn't work after that.
My father had his office in a room in the apartment.
And I remember that he had often clients who were sitting in the hall waiting for him.
Well, it's difficult to say, because I had-- I was still very small.
But I would say that the majority of the-- of their friends-- of the circle of their friends and acquaintances they had were Jewish-- Jewish assimilated, as I said, but Jewish.
They had few non-Jewish friends.
But how-- I mean, people that my father knew from school and from university and people that I met later as well.
And one of them was quite important during the war, because he helped us during the war as well.
But I would say that those were rather smaller part of their acquaintances.
Not before the war.
No.
No, there was nothing of the kind.
No.
Not before the war.
I don't think I remember anything of that.

The only thing that I remember is that my father would on the Yom Kippur, which is about the only day that I am-- that I really remember that he took part-- that he went to the synagogue-- the main synagogue in Warsaw, which was in Tlomackiem, which was a big impressive building.
But it was a kind of a most-- how should I put it?
Most unorthodox-- no unorthodox.

It was orthodox, of course, but it was the more progressive type of synagogue in Warsaw, the one to which those Jews went who had-- who didn't belong permanently to a small synagogue where they would be coming practically almost every day.
So that one was-- I think people were coming there more at high holy days and so on.
So he went there on Yom Kippur.
That's what I remember, but other than that, hardly.
And the other vocations or anything else that you remember from your life?
Did you go on vacations or stay?
Yes, yes.
We went.
We went.
We went but before the war, we went to-- well, we must have gone to near Warsaw as well.
But we went to the sea almost every year, to Sopot which is-- which is near Gdansk, near Danzig on the Baltic Sea.
And it was a well-known-- well-known resort-- and we spent-- we went there, I think, more or less every year.

At least, I remember in 19-- probably 1937-- and probably as well in 1938 and one would-- one would rent a room or a number of rooms there and stay there for this in the summer, usually for probably for at least a month or maybe even two months.
Usually one went, because after the war, we still continued going to Sopot.
And that was usually we went away for the whole two months for July/August.
And we went as well to other places.
There was a place called Orla, which was-- which is not very far from Warsaw, about maybe 50 kilometers or 60 kilometers.
And that was a kind of a-- that was a village in a very beautiful place on the banks of a little river.
And we went there, I think, once in '38 probably.
And then I was very, very ill with diphtheria in 1939.
After I recovered, we-- my parents rented a house there, and we went there to spend the summer.
But my parents traveled as well.
They traveled elsewhere.
They went to France and to Italy and so on.
But I didn't travel abroad.
Not yet.
Not yet.
I think we were meeting very often.
But I remember only a few occasions.
I remember one occasion when my-- one of my mother's sisters, who was a doctor, who was a pediatrician, a child doctor, she married rather late in-- I think it was 1938.
And I remember that's-- or maybe it was '37.
I might have been four then.
And I remember that I was in the synagogue.
I remember the lights and many people.
And later on, there was a wedding reception in [POLISH], which was the street where my grandparents-- my maternal grandparents lived.
But unfortunately, I ate something but then I was not allowed to stay there.
I think out of the excitement, I was-- I became sick, and I was not allowed to remain.
And I was taken back home with-- being very unhappy about it.
And those are basically-- there were other occasions.
I remember all kinds of-- couple of birthdays and so on of my family, of my cousin on my-- I had quite a number of cousins.
So there must have been many occasions like that, but only a few are strongly in my mind.
Very protected, yes.
Yes.
Not-- I wouldn't say that it was entirely happy, because I was a bit of a fragile sickly child.
I always was suffering from all kinds of complaints, which was not very clear what it was.
People thought-- doctors thought it could have been appendicitis.
And I was quite often seen by a doctor, but that must have been just some extra sensitivity.
But other than that, of course, it was a very protected, very, very easy childhood.
Yes.
There were some.
Yes, I remember, but not terribly-- not terribly-- I remember a few names but not-- I wouldn't say that it left on me a very strong mark then.
Yes.
Well, in '39-- in '39, I said that my memory was-- until the war was is sporadic-- it's not-- it's not continuous.
There are certain things which I remember from before the war, but they are not-- not a continuous memory.
But once in '39-- when I was in '39, in summer I was six.
And that almost is-- I was six when on the-- at the end of August.
And from the-- and the war began a few days later.
So really that was the point at which the memory becomes much more continuous and things as well became much more dramatic.
So it was a complete change, in a way.
And what happened was that when the war broke out, we were outside Warsaw.
We were in Orla.
We were in the summer place.
And then for a few days there was, in my memory, complete chaos.
We went back to Warsaw.
And then we left Warsaw and went somewhere near Warsaw, probably because there were air attacks, which were there from the very first day.
And then we came back to Warsaw, and I think my parents didn't know 100% what to do to start.
So my mother started buying in great hurry provisions.
There were sacks of sugar and sacks of flour and things like that being bought and being brought to the apartment.
Although, of course, later it transpired that it was all completely unnecessary, because there were no shortages of sugar, at least not in the immediately.
And there was no need for that.
And then there were continuous air attacks.

And I think that around the beginning of about on the 6th of September, I think, there were some communique on the radio which asked the, I think, the main population of Warsaw to leave the city and to move east, to evacuate the city practically.
You mean all the males.
All the men.
Not--
No, no, no.
It was-- it was just everybody.
It was not-- it was just on the radio.
And anyway, I think that my parents decided then that they were going to leave Warsaw and to move east.
Nobody understood yet.
Of course, nobody had any idea how quickly the war was going to develop.
But then they felt that we are safer in the east of Poland, some hundreds of kilometers more away from the German army.
So my father bought a car, which he didn't buy before the war.
And we-- that was a small car, a Czech car, Tatra, with about-- a car with four places and a small ordinary salon.
And we all squeezed into that car with difficulty, because we squeezed-- there were all together nine people.
My parents and I and my father's sister with her husband and child, my cousin, and another-- and a brother of my father, again with wife and child.
So all together, nine people, which was probably quite difficult and stinks as well.
And we left Warsaw and started driving east.

The brother of my father was an excellent driver, so we went on, although the-- it was impossible to drive at any speed, because the roads were totally clogged with traffic, with pedestrians, with horse carts, with cars, with lorries.
It was all the kind of a picture like one could see later on in films and so on.
And moreover, we were all the time strafed and attacked by plane.
There were German planes who were attacking and shooting at on the road.
So we were extremely lucky to have survived that anyway.
But it took days.
We were-- we were going for days and days.
I don't remember when we were sleeping there on that occasion.
But after about three or four days, we arrived in Brzesc nad Bugiem.
We crossed the River Bug.
And we went further east in Volenia and finally there was-- the petrol that we had was completely finished.
And we then-- on one occasion, my father and his brother, I think, went to a village and hired horses, which would pull the car for another 20 or so kilometers to the nearest town.
And then they-- and then they managed to get some small amount of petrol, with which we finally got after some eight or nine days to Rowne, which is where a cousin of my brother-- of my father lived.
OK.
